text: The surgeon performs a medial to lateral dissection, approaching the root of the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA). The inferior mesenteric vein is visible, and the surgeon identifies a low takeoff of the left colic branch, prompting a pivot to dissect higher to accurately locate the IMA.

The surgeon finalizes the dissection of the inferior mesenteric vein (IMV), noting the parallel course of the left colic artery alongside the IMV. The vessel sealer is used to cauterize and divide the IMV, though it initially sticks to the vessel, requiring a change to a new sealer. The dissection proceeds from medial to lateral up to the splenic flexure without entering the lesser sac. The sigmoid colon is mobilized, and the left-sided dissection is completed to connect with the medial to lateral dissection on the right side. The uterus is suspended using a Keith needle in preparation for the total mesorectal excision (TME).

The surgeon uses scissors positioned on the right to dissect as low as possible, encountering the right iliac vessels. Instruments are repositioned to the patient's left, with the tip elevated to retract the rectum cephalad and assist in retracting the uterus and vagina. The posterior dissection is taken down as low as possible before addressing anterior attachments. The lateral mobilization is completed, followed by anterior dissection. The tumor is located just below the peritoneal reflection on the pouch of Douglas, with visible scarring indicating an excellent response to neoadjuvant therapy.

The surgeon navigates the constricted and scarred area where the upper part of the tumor was located. They carefully dissect the thin septum between the vagina and rectum, avoiding entry into the vagina while maintaining oncologic integrity. The dissection proceeds anteriorly and posteriorly around the rectum, using a rolled-up Raytech for mesorectum retraction. The surgeon employs a suction irrigator to manage smoke from cautery, enhancing visibility with a thirty-degree scope to mobilize the mesentery deep in the pelvis.

The surgeon completes the anterior dissection, carefully peeling the rectocele away from the vagina to avoid catching it with the stapler. Two blue loads are used with a 45-millimeter stapler to deepen the pelvis, taking advantage of the wide female pelvis for easier access.

The surgeon defines the mesenteric pedicle and advances to the proximal resection margin, clearing it off. An abbreviated fan steel incision is made to exteriorize the specimen, place the anvil externally, and then return it internally. The surgeon completes the procedure laparoscopically, using a 33mm EEA stapler to join the ends of the colon, ensuring no twisting of the colon or mesentery. A rigid proctoscopy is performed, followed by a leak test. Images of the specimen are shown, confirming no residual cancer and intact mesentery with a distal margin of 4.2 centimeters.
